questions.title: Testlet 1
questions.scenario:
A client comes with a prescription for rehabilitation for a right-shoulder impingement syndrome. The physician asks that the athletic trainer evaluate and treat as needed. The following is the history of this injury:
     -It is insidious in onset after playing tennis.
     -It started about a month prior.
     -The client plays tennis three times per week for at least an hour.
     -The client has no serious health issues.
     -The client does not take any medications.
     -There is a dull ache in the shoulder, especially when the patient sleeps on their side.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer moves the patient's arm to 90° abduction, 90° horizontal adduction, and glenohumeral internal rotation. Which of the following tests describes this motion? Choose all that apply.

The athletic trainer decides to assess postural alignment from the lateral view. Using a plumb line from the lateral view, which postural landmarks, when misaligned, will contribute to the impingement syndrome? Choose all that apply.
choices: External auditory meatus anterior to the plumb line
Lower cervical vertebral bodies anterior to the plumb line
Acromion process posterior to plumb line
Manubrium posterior to the plumb line

questions.prompt: Read the patient information. Given this information, the athletic trainer must decide on the rehabilitation progression. What instructions and actions should be part of the initial phase of rehabilitation? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The athletic trainer now has the following information:
     -(+) Hawkins-Kennedy test
     -(+)Forward head
     -(+)Kyphosis
     -(+)Hypomobile thoracic spine
     -(+) Weak posterior thoracic musculature
     -(+) Tight anterior thoracic musculature
     -(+) Pain in the subacromial space when the arm is overhead
choices: Place the athlete in a sling.
Avoid lying on the injured shoulder.

hs.alt: Side view of a bare upper body and trunk used to identify where capacitive shortwave diathermy pads should be placed.

questions.prompt: What are the criteria for this patient to return to full activity? Choose all that apply.
choices: Patient has a full range of motion.
Patient agrees for preventive follow-up program.
Patient has improved strength of the rotator cuff and scapular musculature.

questions.title: Testlet 2
questions.scenario: The athletic trainer has set up pre-participation physical examinations for 18- to 21-year-old athletes. As athletes move through the examination stations, the athletic trainer needs to demonstrate competency in a variety of areas.

questions.prompt: One station in the examination requires the athletic trainer to take the athletes' blood pressure. What is the correct order of steps to follow to obtain a blood pressure measurement? Drag the list items into the correct order.

tokens: Place the stethoscope over the brachial artery at the cubital fossa.
Deflate bladder at 3 mmHg per second.

zones.accept: Place the stethoscope over the brachial artery at the cubital fossa.
Deflate bladder at 3 mmHg per second.

At one station, the athletic trainer must check each athlete's pulse at the following arteries:

questions.prompt: During the examinations, the athletic trainer makes general observations of each athlete, including any unusual skin color. Match the skin color with the likely condition.

choices: History of repeated episodes of diarrhea

questions.title: Testlet 3
questions.scenario: You are an athletic trainer starting a new position at the university level. As part of your responsibilities, you are charged with managing risk by enhancing the pre-participation physical examination process. Answer the following questions regarding this process.

questions.prompt: With whom should the historical personal and family medical history provided by an athlete be reviewed? Choose all that apply.
choices: Whenever the athlete becomes ill or injured

questions.prompt: Occasionally, an abnormality or condition is found that may limit an athlete's participation or predispose them to further injury. In these cases, which of the following questions should the team physician review when the athlete's ability to meet the criteria for participation is being determined? Choose all that apply.
choices: Does the condition pose an unacceptable risk or place the athlete at increased risk for further injury?
Does the condition place other participants at risk of injury?
Can the athlete safely participate with treatment (e.g., medication, rehabilitation, bracing, padding)?
Can limited participation be allowed while treatment is being completed?
Can the athlete afford testing to determine medical eligibility?

questions.prompt:
During the musculoskeletal pre-participation physical examination screening, what would the physician be looking for during each of the movements below? Drag each answer to the correct numbered movement.
1. Patient stands facing the examiner
2. Duck walking
3. Patient stands with their back to the examiner
4. Knees straight, and they touch their toes
5. Abduction of the shoulder to 90 degrees
6. Total external rotation of the arms
7. Shoulder shrug

tokens: Scoliosis and hip range of motion
Flexibility of the hamstrings and lower back
Range of motion at the shoulder

zones.accept: Scoliosis and hip range of motion
Flexibility of the hamstrings and lower back
Range of motion at the shoulder

questions.prompt: When is noninvasive cardiac testing (e.g., ECG, echocardiography, exercise stress testing) recommended? Choose all that apply.
choices: For all football and basketball players
Not considered a routine aspect of pre-participation screening
For those with a personal or family history of cardiac issues

questions.prompt: What parts of the pre-participation physical exam should be included for an athlete? Choose all that apply.
choices: Confirmation of sickle cell trait status
Thorough review of personal and family history of diabetes mellitus

questions.title: Testlet 4
questions.scenario: Allison is a junior at Dublin Area High School and is a starter on the girls' basketball team. Although Allison is not a captain, she is one of the better players, averaging 22 points per game, 10 assists, and 14 rebounds per game. She is a very pleasant young lady, is in the National Honor Society, and is near the top of her class with a 3.92 GPA. Allison would like to study medicine, but does not know what area specifically she would like to focus on. Recently, she suffered from a mild sprained ankle. She missed the game yesterday and is likely to miss the next two games due to her injury. The athletic trainer has cared for all of Allison's injuries and knows her and her family well. Allison has even done some babysitting for the athletic trainer. Since the injury, she has not been her usual self. Allison has seemed "sad" or depressed, a little more irritable, and does not feel as if she is part of the basketball team since she is not playing. The athletic trainer is not positive but thinks the smell of alcohol has been on or around Allison the day before yesterday and again today.

questions.prompt: Allison smelled of alcohol a few days ago, but the athletic trainer was not sure. What question(s) does the athletic trainer need to ask regarding their concerns about alcohol? Choose all that apply.
choices: Is there anything other than the injury that is bothering you?
Have you talked about any of this with your parents?
Allison, the other day I thought I smelled alcohol on you, but I was not sure. Again today, I smell it again. I understand you are really stressed, but we need to talk about this. What is going on?

questions.prompt: Read the patient's history. What questions would you ask related to the cut, annoyed, guilty, and eye (CAGE) mnemonic? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The history of the patient reveals the following:
     -The patient failed her class exam.
     -Her boyfriend wants to take a break.
     -She cannot play because of an ankle sprain.
     -The patient is defensive but admits to drinking.
choices: Have you ever used a nightcap to help you sleep in the evening?
Have you ever felt angry or annoyed when someone asks or confronts you about your alcohol use?
Have you changed your use of alcohol to hide it from others?
Have you ever used an eye-opener of alcohol early in the morning?
Have you ever tried to cut down on your alcohol use?
Have you ever tried to use alcohol to forget about negative events?
Have you ever felt guilty about your substance use?
Have you ever felt like alcohol makes you happy?

questions.prompt: What are the key concepts to have a successful intervention when dealing with someone who is involved in substance abuse? Choose all that apply.
choices: The individual's willingness to find alternative coping strategies to deal with their stress
Involvement in treatment by family and friends
Dual diagnoses of substance abuse and comorbid conditions

questions.prompt: Several comorbidities may accompany substance abuse. Which of the following are comorbid conditions that may accompany one's substance abuse? Choose all that apply.
choices: Restless, fidgety, or always "on the go"
Difficulty in engaging in quiet activities

questions.prompt: Several comorbidities may accompany substance abuse. Which of the following are comorbid conditions that may accompany one's substance abuse? Choose all that apply.
choices: Impulsive behavior (e.g., clowning around, unnecessarily touching others)
Difficulty in following through on a goal
Falls (especially in older adults or the elderly)

questions.prompt: Which of the following health care professionals are qualified to assess an individual with a substance abuse issue? Choose all that apply.
choices: A certified drug and alcohol abuse counselor

questions.title: Testlet 5
questions.scenario: In the athletic trainer's observation, a young tennis player who is normally very outgoing is no longer engaged with the team. The athletic trainer notices lethargy, loss of weight, and poor performance, and the athlete seems to be going through the motions at practice.

questions.prompt: When taking history from this athlete using the history, observation, planning, evaluation (HOPE) protocol, what questions should be asked? Choose all that apply.
choices: When did you start feeling this way?
How often do you feel this way?
Have you felt like this before?
Do you have a plan for suicide?
Is there a history of mental illness in your family?
How long have you been playing tennis?
What are your signs of illness or injury?
What are your symptoms of illness or injury?

questions.prompt: Read the patient's history. What are the keys to determining if a patient has burned out using the Maslach Burnout Inventory? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The history reveals the patient is experiencing the following:
     -Chronic fatigue, with emotional exhaustion
     -A hard time sleeping and has lost some weight
     -Low self-esteem and low motivation for sport
     -A hard time concentrating
choices: Lack of a sense of responsibility

questions.prompt: This patient needs a referral to a specialist. Which individuals should the athletic trainer consider referring this patient to? Choose all that apply.
What are the most common treatments for sports burnout? Choose all that apply.
choices: Communicate with athletes, coaches, and athletic trainers
Get feedback from athletes, coaches, and athletic staff on the physiological effects of daily training

questions.prompt: What are the most common treatments for sports burnout? Choose all that apply.
choices: Develop psychological skills such as relaxation
Set short-term goals for games and practices
Begin practices earlier, but for shorter durations of time

questions.title: Testlet 6
questions.scenario: A patient complains that they have a headache. They do not recall any trauma to their head that would cause a headache.

questions.prompt: Look at the figures and identify the form of headache described by dragging the answer to the correct blank.

image.alt: Four headache illustrations with captions describing sinus, cluster, tension, and migraine headache patterns.

questions.prompt: What items might cause a headache? Choose all that apply.
What are some risk factors for headaches? Choose all that apply.
Which of the following warrants referral to a physician for headaches? Choose all that apply.

questions.title: Testlet 7
questions.scenario: The athletic trainer has been called to the locker room. There is an athlete lying on the floor. The athlete says they have not been feeling well for a couple of days. The athlete says they are nauseous and have pain when they cough. They have gastrointestinal issues, but they can pinpoint nothing.

questions.prompt: Which of the following should be a part of history to determine illness or injury? Choose all that apply.
choices: Do a mental health status check
Determine onset and severity of symptoms
Determine the history of drug abuse

questions.prompt: Read the patient information. Which areas of the body should the athletic trainer visually inspect? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The patient has shared the following information:
     -(+) No physical trauma
     -(+) Some pain in the periumbilical area, but not defined
     -(-) Mental health or drug use issues
     -(+) Lack of desire to eat
     -(+) Nausea
     -(+) Feels warmer than normal

questions.prompt: Read the visual inspection. Which analysis should the athletic trainer do at this point? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The visual inspection reveals the following:
     -(-) Muscle tone is normal
     -(+) Patient feels more comfortable with knees and hips flexed
     -(-) Throat
     -(-) Skin

questions.prompt: Read the patient assessment. Which of the following injuries or illnesses should be suspected based on the signs and symptoms provided? Choose all that apply.
questions.supporting_text:
The patient assessment reveals the following information:
     -(+) McBurney's point
     -(+)Dunphy's sign
     -(+) Rovsing's sign
     -(-) Throat visual

questions.prompt: What is the immediate treatment for this condition? Choose all that apply.
choices: Check the sclera of the eye

questions.title: Testlet 8
questions.scenario: The team physician has asked the athletic trainer to cast a nondisplaced lateral malleolus ankle fracture.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are the proper materials to select to cast an ankle fracture? Choose all that apply.

image.alt: Roll of white stockinette for cast application.
Roll of white fiberglass casting tape beside a sealed foil package.
Box of 4-inch Scotchcast conformable roll splint next to a folded sheet of padding.
Two small rolls of white undercast padding.
Metal bandage scissors with a blunt tip.
Elastic bandage being wrapped around an ankle and foot.
Clinician applying a white short leg cast to a patient's lower leg.

questions.prompt: The athletic trainer is to create a cast out of fiberglass that is a non-weight-bearing short leg cast. Which of the following is a proper non-weight-bearing short leg cast? Choose all that apply.

image.alt: Rigid walking boot with straps extending from foot to below the knee.
White short leg cast extending from below the knee to the foot.
Elevated lower leg wearing a white short leg cast with the knee bent.
Clinician supporting a patient wearing a bright pink long leg cast extending from foot to above the knee.

questions.prompt: When removing the cast with an oscillating cast saw, what are the best techniques in order? Drag the list items into the correct order.

tokens: Place the cast saw perpendicular to the surface.
Apply downward pressure until there is decreased resistance.
Move the cast saw a little more each time, the length of the cast.
Cut a longitudinal line on both sides of the cast.
Open the cast with a cast spreader.
Use bandage scissors to cut through roller bandages and stockinette.

zones.accept: Place the cast saw perpendicular to the surface.
Apply downward pressure until there is decreased resistance.
Move the cast saw a little more each time, the length of the cast.
Cut a longitudinal line on both sides of the cast.
Open the cast with a cast spreader.
Use bandage scissors to cut through roller bandages and stockinette.

questions.title: Testlet 9
questions.scenario: An athlete comes in with some sort of rash on his cheek.

questions.prompt: Looking at the photo, what kind of skin infection do you suspect?

image.alt: Close-up of grouped pearly skin papules with central umbilication.

questions.prompt: What signs and symptoms would the athletic trainer look for in a patient to distinguish between herpes and another skin infection? Choose all that apply.
When recording verbal physician's orders for medication, what information should be documented? Choose all that apply.
choices: Have the physician cosign when possible

questions.prompt: What substance should be used for disinfecting the athletic training facility? Choose all that apply.
choices: Hospital-strength disinfectant approved by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)
Disinfectants approved by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)

questions.prompt: What are the criteria for returning this patient to competition? Choose all that apply.
choices: Patient has taken antivirals for at least five days.
The patient will wear an occlusive dressing over the lesions.
The athlete has no new lesions for at least three days.
No other athletes have come down with the virus.
The athlete has to shower post-practice.
The mats and equipment must be disinfected daily.

questions.title: Testlet 10
questions.scenario: A volleyball player enters the athletic training clinic four days after an emergency department visit to address an open wound (no stitches) and collateral ligament sprain caused by a fall on concrete, non-athletic associated.

questions.prompt: To address the open wound and ligament sprain, which of the following therapeutic modalities may be used? Choose all that apply
Light-emitting diode (LED) was selected to address the wound healing of the finger. Why should the LED be the modality of choice? Choose all that apply.
choices: Research indicates that LED stimulates mast cells
Research indicates greater wound healing outcomes than low-level lasers
Research indicates increased levels of new blood vessels
Research indicates greater wound healing outcomes
Research indicates equal healing rates to low-level lasers
Research indicates significantly better fibroblast stimulation than low-level lasers

questions.prompt: The patient is two weeks post-injury with sufficient wound healing. What is the best way to warm the finger and hand before therapeutic rehabilitation? Choose all that apply.
choices: Paraffin bath using a dipping technique

questions.prompt: Arrange the first six steps of the treatment protocol for the paraffin bath in the appropriate order for a dipping technique. Drag the items into the correct order.

tokens: The temperature should be checked for an appropriate temperature of 126 °F (52 °C).
The patient should wash their hands.
Guide the hand into the paraffin, making sure the patient does not contact the bottom.
Hold the hand in the paraffin bath for 2 to 3 seconds.
Remove the hand from the paraffin bath.

zones.accept: The temperature should be checked for an appropriate temperature of 126 °F (52 °C).
The patient should wash their hands.
Guide the hand into the paraffin, making sure the patient does not contact the bottom.
Hold the hand in the paraffin bath for 2 to 3 seconds.
Remove the hand from the paraffin bath.

questions.prompt: Arrange the remaining five steps of the treatment protocol for the paraffin bath in the appropriate order for a dipping technique. Drag the items into the correct order.

tokens: Repeat dipping half the number of the designated treatment.
Place a plastic bag over the area, followed by an oven mitt or hydrocollator pads.
Check the patient's response after the first 5 minutes.
Leave it on for 20 to 30 minutes.
The temperature should be raised to 212 °F (100 °C) to kill any bacteria.

zones.accept: Repeat dipping half the number of the designated treatment.
Place a plastic bag over the area, followed by an oven mitt or hydrocollator pads.
Check the patient's response after the first 5 minutes.
Leave it on for 20 to 30 minutes.
The temperature should be raised to 212 °F (100 °C) to kill any bacteria.

questions.prompt: In the absence of a paraffin bath, a warm whirlpool was selected. What contraindications prevent the use of this thermotherapy five days post-emergency department visit? Choose all that apply.

questions.title: Testlet 11
questions.scenario: You are out to dinner with the team, and one of your coaches starts coughing and then grabs their throat. The patient is conscious.

questions.prompt: What is the correct order in working with this patient? Drag the items into the correct order.

tokens: See if the scene is safe.
Ask the patient if they can speak, cough, or if they are choking.
Have someone call 9-1-1 and bring the AED

zones.accept: See if the scene is safe.
Ask the patient if they can speak, cough, or if they are choking.
Have someone call 9-1-1 and bring the AED

questions.prompt: In what instances would you use chest thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts? Choose all that apply.
choices: The patient is taller than you.
The patient is shorter than you.
The patient is overweight, and you cannot reach around them to give abdominal thrusts.

questions.prompt: The coach becomes unconscious and slumps to the floor. What are the next steps to supporting an unconscious complete airway obstruction when the person shows no signs of life? Drag the items into the proper order to treat this patient.

tokens: Roll the patient to the supine position if necessary.
Look, listen, and feel for breathing (no air flow).
Give two rescue breaths (air does not make the chest rise or fall).
Give two rescue breaths (air does not make the chest rise or fall).
Check the mouth for an obstruction.

zones.accept: Roll the patient to the supine position if necessary.
Look, listen, and feel for breathing (no air flow).
Give two rescue breaths (air does not make the chest rise or fall).
Give two rescue breaths (air does not make the chest rise or fall).
Check the mouth for an obstruction.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the proper position for chest compressions? Choose all that apply.
choices: Shoulders should be over the center of the chest.
Place the heel of the hand on the center of the chest.
Place a fist on the center of the chest, thumb side on the chest.
Kneel at the side of the patient parallel to the patient.
Kneel at the side of the patient perpendicular to the patient.

questions.prompt: Which best describes how compressions are given? Choose all that apply.
choices: Give compressions at a rate of 60 per minute.
Give compressions at a rate of 100 per minute.
Make sure the depth of compression is 1 inch.
Make sure the depth of compression is 1.5 to 2 inches.
Make sure each compression is distinctive in an attempt to remove the object.

questions.prompt: You have dislodged the object and removed it. The patient is still unconscious. What are the next steps for this patient? Choose all that apply.
choices: Place the patient in the recovery position.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an agency from which a clinic- or hospital-based sports medicine program should consider obtaining accreditation during program development?
choices: Joint Review Committee on Accreditation of Health Care Organizations (JRCAHO)

questions.prompt: What is a plan for expressing the organization's intended behavior relative to a specific program subfunction?
What is the budget process that requires the manager to justify every expense without reference to previous spending patterns?
What are the items in a budget that involve renovations, additions, or modifications to facilities?
Which of the following is critical to increase efficiency in a sports medicine program's budgeting process?
What is one of the main tasks in operating a successful for-profit sports medicine program?
choices: Charging high fees to solicit revenue for cash flow
Planning and implementing a marketing strategy
Copying the strategies used by nearby providers
Remaining open for services 24 hours a day
Hiring only new graduates to keep overhead personnel costs minimized

questions.prompt: What is the government regulation that requires educational institutions to receive formal written consent from students before educational records are released to a third party?
choices: Health Insurance Portability and Privacy Act (HIPAA)
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)

questions.prompt: What are the physician directions provided in writing to an athletic trainer to outline interventions in the absence of a physician?
Which type of medical insurance system eliminates the need for an institution to file insurance claim forms?
In what group is a health care provider participating, if it involves a health insurance plan in which the provider receives a fixed fee for services rendered to the members of a specific group?
What is the term for liability-generating conduct associated with an adverse outcome of patient treatment?
What does the policy that addresses the support needed by patients and their families, friends, roommates, and teammates following a traumatic life encounter refer to?
Under what grounds is a drug test conducted when an individual displays signs of drug use?
Which of the following is most true regarding emergency action plans?
choices: The written plan should be as short as possible.
Parents should always be in charge of enacting an emergency action plan at a high school event.
Coaches should not be trained in CPR and the use of an AED.
An emergency action plan can be copied from those of other schools to save time in developing one.
Emergency action plans should be venue-specific.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements about the glycemic index of foods is correct?
choices: Low glycemic-index foods include less protein and fiber than high glycemic-index foods.
The glycemic index of foods was initially developed to help athletes.
High glycemic-index foods may help endurance athletes perform better by sustaining their energy during long bouts of exercise.
High glycemic-index foods are best consumed immediately after exercise to rapidly refuel muscles.

questions.prompt: Which group of athletes has the highest risk of developing iron-deficiency anemia?
choices: Teenage athletes, especially girls who are growing quickly
Vegetarians who eat iron-enriched breakfast cereal

questions.prompt: As one ages, what generally shows a decrease in the number?
What among the following would be an example of the inability of the hamstring to perform a ROM past 120° of knee flexion during a hamstring curl?

metadata.note: Finalized practice test JSON after manual review of answers, images, hotspot, matching, and drag-and-drop items.
metadata.image_manifest_scope: distinct image references currently used by questions
